risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "allocation will be determined through an external telephone randomisation service at the Clinical Trials Unit of the Manchester Academic Health Science Centre. Clusters will be submitted to the randomisation service in pairs... One member of the pair will be allocated to intervention by random selection, the other allocated to control." Comment: This describes a central, external randomisation process with random selection within matched pairs, which is sufficient to indicate a truly random allocation sequence was used.

Quote: "To eliminate selection bias, allocation will be determined through an external telephone randomisation service at the Clinical Trials Unit of the Manchester Academic Health Science Centre." Comment: This is an explicit central allocation mechanism (telephone randomisation service) that would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment, so allocation concealment is at low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The EQUIP training intervention consists of 2 days face-to-face training..." and "All consenting CMHTs allocated to the intervention arm will then receive the EQUIP training." Also, "It will be explained to service users that their CMHT is involved in a study to test the effectiveness of a new training package ... compared to the usual care planning experience." Comment: Participants and the professionals delivering care could not be blinded to whether their CMHT received special training versus usual care, and this knowledge could plausibly affect the patient-reported outcomes used in this trial (e.g., perceived autonomy support, involvement, satisfaction, wellbeing). Therefore, the dominant risk for performance bias is high.

Quote: "To reduce detection bias, we will seek to blind researchers undertaking assessments of the quality of care planning to the allocation. We will report the success or otherwise of our attempts at blinding." Comment: The protocol states that researchers assessing quality of care planning were intended to be blinded, but it does not clearly report whether blinding was actually achieved or maintained. Most key outcomes are patient-reported self-report questionnaires (e.g. HCCQ-10, PROM, VSSS-EU-54, GASS, WEMWBS, DREEM, HADS, CALPAS, WHOQOL-BREF, EQ-5D-5L), so the participant is effectively the outcome assessor; however, the paper does not state whether participants were blinded, and for the mixed set of outcomes there is insufficient explicit information to confidently classify overall detection bias as low or high.

Quote: "Analysis of outcomes will follow intention-to-treat principles: outcome data will be sought and included in the analysis for all service users irrespective of receipt of the intervention or completion of care planning during the time scale of the EQUIP trial." Comment: The protocol indicates an ITT approach with efforts to obtain outcome data for all participants, and it anticipates an 80% follow-up rate (i.e., reasonable attrition) without suggesting major imbalance or outcome-related dropout. This makes attrition bias unlikely, so Low risk is appropriate.
